summary: Joe Reis reflects on the awkward timing of Snowflake and Databricks conferences being scheduled in the same week, forcing the data community to choose sides in what feels like a vendor-driven wedge. He argues that big vendor conferences prioritize selling over community needs, and calls for more practitioner-focused, vendor-neutral data engineering conferences.
key_points: Snowflake and Databricks scheduled conferences the same week, creating logistical and financial burdens for attendees and sponsors
Big vendor conferences prioritize product sales over genuine community building and practitioner needs
The data community deserves standalone, vendor-neutral conferences focused on practitioners rather than sales pitches
Low-key events and after-hours gatherings often provide more authentic community connection than scripted conference content
money_quotes: It felt like somebody forced a wedge in the middle of the data community, and it made for awkward and expensive decisions for sponsors and attendees.
The major data platforms are in an arms race, which is expected behavior. Kill or be killed.
We get the community we deserve. We deserve better.

One of these conferences asked me to give a talk about products they want to push. On top of that, timelines were super aggressive for slides to be submitted 'because legal review,' AND they weren't comping my speaking fee and travel. Since I'm not a charity, don't enter into lopsided relationships, and I'm in the middle of many paid projects, I decided this wasn't a good fit for me.
key_insight: The data community is being fragmented by vendor competition rather than unified around practitioner needs, and it's time for community-led, vendor-neutral conferences to fill that gap.
